headline: CRUDE SPIKES 4%, MILK EXPLODES 7% OVERNIGHT
subheadline: Energy rally pressures input costs while dairy surge creates livestock feed dynamic shift.
lead: Crude oil's unexpected 4% overnight rally to $99.31 signals rising input costs just as spring field prep begins, while Class III milk's shocking 7% jump to $17.30 reshuffles livestock feed economics. Corn managed a modest 9¢ gain to $4.67, but the real story is how energy and dairy volatility will ripple through your spring planting decisions.
teaser: Energy costs surge as planting season arrives — but dairy's massive move changes everything.

one_number.context: Crude's 4% overnight spike puts oil within striking distance of $100, spelling trouble for diesel and fertilizer costs just as spring fieldwork ramps up. Every $10 move in crude translates to roughly 25¢ per gallon in diesel — money that comes straight out of your planting budget.

sections.title: GRAINS & OILSEEDS
sections.body: Corn's 9¢ rally to $4.67 looks modest until you factor in crude oil's surge driving up production costs. December corn at $4.91 maintains a healthy 24¢ carry, suggesting the market still expects adequate supplies despite pre-planting jitters. Wheat jumped 11¢ to $6.13 on continued Black Sea export concerns, while soybeans slipped slightly despite meal strength. The disconnect between energy costs rising and grain prices staying relatively calm won't last — something has to give.
sections.bottom_line: Corn holding steady despite rising input costs — temporary calm before the storm.
sections.farmer_action: Consider locking diesel prices now before crude breaks $100 — grain prices haven't caught up to input cost reality yet.

sections.title: LIVESTOCK & DAIRY
sections.body: Class III milk's explosive 7% overnight rally to $17.30 caught everyone off guard, creating a massive shift in feed-to-milk ratios. This surge makes dairy operations significantly more profitable relative to beef cattle, which dropped 0.3% to $225.95. Lean hogs held steady at $86.00, but with corn prices stable and milk skyrocketing, dairy cows just became the most profitable ruminants in the barn. Feeder cattle's slight gain suggests some optimism, but they're fighting an uphill battle against dairy economics.
sections.bottom_line: Dairy just became king — milk prices make corn look like a bargain.
sections.farmer_action: If you're considering livestock expansion, dairy operations now offer significantly better margins than beef.

sections.title: ENERGY & INPUTS
sections.body: Crude oil's 4% overnight spike to $99.31 is the real story today, threatening to push diesel and fertilizer costs higher just as spring applications begin. Natural gas dropping 3.3% to $3.13 provides some relief on nitrogen costs, but that won't offset crude's impact on diesel and phosphate transport. The dollar's 0.8% strengthening makes imported fertilizers slightly cheaper, but crude's momentum suggests input cost inflation is about to accelerate. This timing couldn't be worse with fieldwork starting.
sections.bottom_line: Input cost squeeze tightening — lock in what you can now.
sections.farmer_action: Prioritize diesel and liquid fertilizer purchases this week before crude breaks $100 — prices only go one way from here.

sections.title: MACRO & TRADE
sections.body: The dollar's 0.8% overnight strength to 100.53 makes U.S. exports less competitive but provides some offset to crude oil's surge for import costs. Gold's 1.9% drop and silver's 5% collapse suggest money is rotating out of safe havens into energy plays, confirming crude's move isn't just technical. The S&P 500's 0.6% decline shows markets are nervous about energy inflation returning. For grain exports, a stronger dollar creates headwinds just as South American harvest competition intensifies.
sections.bottom_line: Strong dollar hurts exports while energy inflation threatens margins.
sections.farmer_action: No immediate action — but watch for export demand to soften if dollar strength continues.

the_more_you_know.title: Why Crude Oil Moves Matter More Than Grain Prices Right Now
the_more_you_know.body: Every $1 move in crude oil impacts your operation more directly than a 2¢ move in corn during planting season. Diesel represents 15-20% of your variable costs, while fertilizer transport costs are directly tied to energy. A $10 crude spike adds roughly $2.50 per acre in diesel costs alone — before you factor in fertilizer price increases. This is why smart farmers lock input costs first, grain prices second during pre-plant.

watch_list.desc: Oil inventory data — another draw could push crude to $100+ and trigger panic buying in diesel markets.
Fertilizer dealer pricing updates — crude's move will flow through to phosphate and potash transport costs.
USDA Prospective Plantings report — acreage intentions become critical if input costs keep rising.
Dollar strength continuation — sustained moves above 101.00 start hurting export competitiveness.

daily_quote.text: Markets can stay irrational longer than you can stay solvent.

source_summary: CME Group · NYMEX · USDA · Federal Reserve · Reuters
